channel.title: +Young non v1 SVs on रामानुजीये विश्वासटिप्पन्यः
channel.description: Recent content in +Young non v1 SVs on रामानुजीये विश्वासटिप्पन्यः

item.title: 01 Introduction: Setting the Stage
item.description:
The past two centuries have been a period of momentous religious, social, and political changes in southern India. These include the Tamil Renaissance, late colonialism in the Madras Presidency, 1 Dravidian nationalism, Dalit mobilisation, the Indian struggle for Independence, and then the new political entity Tamil Nadu.2

At the epicentre of these changes has been an awakening to injustices—those created by colonialism, but also for non-Brahmins, those long-standing ones created by the caste system. Non-Brahmins have dreamed that equality would create better futures for them. They have refashioned the past into new stories through recuperation, retrospection, and images of reform, revolution and utopia. With power shifting, hierarchies re-aligning and caste identities merging or colliding, they have re-imagined their own identities.

item.title: 02 Non-Brahmin Śrīvaiṣṇava Leaders
item.description:
Traces of Non-Brahmin Śrīvaiṣṇava Leaders

Research

My study of Śrīvaiṣṇava non-Brahmins was to be about recent changes in Tamil society. Perhaps it was fortuitous that I wanted to interview some elderly [[Śrī vaiṣṇavas|Śrīvaiṣṇavas]], because their stories would soon be lost to history. My questions about memories of grandparents and parents took me back into late colonial times.

This chapter is about the traces of some Bhāgavata leaders, ones that I discovered just in time—that is, before they are erased forever from the historical record. I searched for more details. Sometimes this took me to village shrines and tombs or memorial temples (samādhis), to photographs near descendants’ household altars, to pamphlets forgotten in personal libraries, and occasionally to a court case. One trace sometimes led to another, such as a booklet containing a short biography. Other vestiges led nowhere. These included many references to published books that no longer exist, even in the libraries of Tamil Nadu.

item.title: 03 Chennai and Beyond
item.description:
Stories from Chennai and Beyond

These oral histories emerged from the heart of Chennai, its surrounding suburbs, nearby towns such as Sriperumbudur, and villages between the hubs. They represent several generations—from ‘seniors’ to students— many castes, and both men and women. Each story expresses a lived experience, which is the raw material from which narratives are fashioned.

The Grandson and the Outcaste Hamlet

The old Ambassador car lurched through yet another pothole. The monsoon had reminded the city once again that natural forces can halt human progress. The pock-marked road wound to the northwestern edge of Chennai’s sprawling suburbs, relentlessly intruding on the paddy fields. Endless storefronts stacked with stainless-steel pots, tires or saris—this is the land of the small businessman—made it impossible to know where one municipality ended and another began. Occasionally, our driver stopped for directions. In a staccato voice, someone listed the turns and gesticulated toward the horizon. Again,

item.title: 04 Puducherry and Beyond
item.description:
Oral Histories from Puducherry and Beyond

I refer loosely to the general area around Puducherry. This region includes the former British area of South Arcot and the former French colony of Pondichéri (traditionally known as Putuvai), which then became Pondicherry. Today, India has redefined South Arcot as the districts of Cuddalore and Villupuram, and Pondicherry as a Union Territory (a special status to recognise its French heritage). This general region is significant for my purposes here, because it was for several centuries an area of Śrīvaiṣṇava proselytism and non-Brahmin leadership in the late nineteenth century. When Pondicherry emerged as the cultural and intellectual metropolis of this area, after joining India in 1954 as a Union Territory, Bhāgavata academics from other regions joined local traditional scholars and literary figures to further the strong non-Brahmin presence in the city, with links to the surrounding villages and the maṭha in Tirukkoyilur.

item.title: 05 Srirangam, Tanjavur
item.description:
five

Tales from Srirangam, Tanjavur and Beyond

K. K. A. Venkatachari arranged interviews for me at Srirangam with non-Brahmin gardeners and Sattadas, as well as one Brahmin ā cā rya with many non-Brahmin disciples, whom I will discuss in the next chapter. Having deep family connections at this famous temple, Venkatachari also arranged for my own private darśana of Lord Raṅganātha— something extremely rare for foreigners. I then understood how the sight of Ra ṅganātha had so thrilled Tiruppāṇāḻvār that he broke into ecstatic song: ‘… My God! His lovely dark body of unfading beauty strung with pearls and big dazzling gems fills my heart!’ (Amalaṉātipiraṉ 9)1.

item.title: 06 Śrīvaiṣṇava Brahmin Voices
item.description: In my interviews with Bhāgavatas, the topic of Brahmins came up many times. Sometimes non-Brahmins viewed them negatively, sometimes positively, sometimes ambivalently. In this chapter, I begin with two Brahmins who lived during colonial times, and whose names had surfaced in my interviews and the biographies of the non-Brahmin leaders I discussed in Chapter 2. After this, I examine the field notes of Raymond Williams, who interviewed ācāryas and their relatives in 1988 and 1991 in order to detect changes that were occurring in their roles.1 Presenting my own interviews, I search for further changes over the past twenty-five years. Finally, I compare my findings with the study of Tamil Brahmins by C.J.Fuller and Haripriya Narasimhan, published in 2014.2

item.title: 07 Temple Politics, Courts
item.description:
Temple Politics and the Courts

Although the temple redistributive system—with roles sanctioned by precedent, common acknowledgement and chains of command—generally worked well for the medieval temple, it was a delicate system and conflicts could arise if boundaries were ignored or usurped. According to Arjun Appadurai, the king might have to intervene discreetly, but that was rare. By colonial times, however, the generally laissez-faire functioning of the temple was changing gradually through codification of what was deemed local rights and privileges, albeit now often arbitrary ones. The Teṉkalais gained the upper hand, even though the [[Va ṭakalais|Vaṭakalais]] had attempted a takeover by manipulating the British predilection for rules, which enabled them to take control of the trusteeship and therefore the temple itself.1

item.title: 08 Big Picture
item.description:
Thinking about the Big Picture

Discussion of the ‘big picture’ must be framed, to some extent, by the fact that [[Ś r ī vaiṣṇ ava|Śrīvaiṣṇava]] praxis and identity today have been shaped by the forces and interpretations of modernity. There are debates over whether modernity has a core definition, such as rationality and autonomy, or whether it is best understood by listing key components, such as industrialisation, science, technology, a market economy, urbanisation, the modern State, democracy, emancipation from exploitation, new collectivities, human rights, universal education, the rule of law, individualism, autonomy, the Internet, and secularism.
